---
title: "007 — EV adoption as contagion on the state network"
date: 2026-06-12
---

## Question

Does EV adoption spread between **neighbouring states** like a contagion?
If seeing EVs next door normalises them (demonstration effects, dealer spillover,
charging corridors), adoption should be spatially clustered and the order in
which states crossed the adoption threshold should be reproducible by a simple
neighbourhood-pressure rule. (Background: see the *EV Contagion* card in the
[experiment guide](guide.qmd).)

## Setup

```{python}
import duckdb
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from complexity_lab.config import settings
from complexity_lab.networks.contagion import (
    fit_tau, load_adjacency, morans_i, observed_adoption_years, seed_influence,
)

con = duckdb.connect(str(settings.db_path), read_only=True)
g = load_adjacency(con)
panel = con.execute(
    "SELECT state_code, year, ev_share FROM panel_state_year WHERE state_code <> 'ALL'"
).df()
latest_full = int(panel["year"].max()) - 1
THRESHOLD = 0.02  # 2% EV share = 'adopted'
print(f"adjacency: {g.number_of_nodes()} states, {g.number_of_edges()} borders; "
      f"latest full year {latest_full}")
```

## 1 — Is EV adoption spatially clustered? (Moran's I)

```{python}
latest = panel[panel["year"] == latest_full].set_index("state_code")["ev_share"]
moran = morans_i(latest, g)
print(f"Moran's I = {moran['I']:.3f} (expected under randomness "
      f"{moran['expected_I']:.3f}), permutation p = {moran['p_value']:.4f}")
```

```{python}
dim = con.execute("SELECT state_code, geojson_name FROM dim_state").df()
m = latest.reset_index().merge(dim, on="state_code")
import json
geo = json.loads(settings.geojson_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
px.choropleth(m, geojson=geo, locations="geojson_name",
              featureidkey="properties.ST_NM", color="ev_share",
              color_continuous_scale="Viridis", fitbounds="locations",
              title=f"EV share by state, {latest_full}").update_geos(visible=False)
```

## 2 — The observed cascade

```{python}
observed = observed_adoption_years(panel, threshold=THRESHOLD)
mo = observed.reset_index().merge(dim, on="state_code")
px.choropleth(mo, geojson=geo, locations="geojson_name",
              featureidkey="properties.ST_NM", color="adoption_year",
              color_continuous_scale="Plasma_r", fitbounds="locations",
              title=f"Year each state crossed {THRESHOLD:.0%} EV share").update_geos(visible=False)
```

## 3 — Can neighbourhood pressure reproduce the order? (τ-sweep)

```{python}
fits = fit_tau(g, observed)
best_tau = float(fits["spearman_rho"].idxmax())
fig = px.line(fits.reset_index(), x="tau", y="spearman_rho", markers=True,
              title="Match between simulated and observed adoption order vs τ")
fig.add_vline(x=best_tau, line_dash="dot",
              annotation_text=f"best τ = {best_tau:.2f}, ρ = {fits['spearman_rho'].max():.2f}")
fig
```

## 4 — Where would you seed a cascade?

```{python}
infl = seed_influence(g, best_tau).head(12).reset_index()
px.bar(infl, x="cascade_size", y="seed", orientation="h",
       hover_data=["mean_step"], title=f"Single-seed cascade size at τ = {best_tau:.2f}")
```

## Finding

```{python}
fastest = observed.dropna().sort_values().head(5)
print("First movers:", ", ".join(f"{s} ({int(y)})" for s, y in fastest.items()))
print(f"Moran's I {moran['I']:.2f} (p={moran['p_value']:.3f}); "
      f"best tau {best_tau:.2f} with rho {fits['spearman_rho'].max():.2f}")
```

A significantly positive Moran's I says EV adoption is geographically clustered,
and a low best-fit τ with high rank correlation says a simple neighbour-pressure
rule reproduces much of the adoption order — both consistent with (not proof of)
spatial contagion. States the model flips **early** but that haven't crossed the
threshold in reality are the watch-list; states reality flipped earlier than the
model are policy-driven jumps (Delhi's EV policy being the canonical case).
The honest caveats: n≈33, common shocks (fuel prices, national subsidies) also
cluster spatially, and adjacency ignores non-spatial channels (media, OEM launch
waves). The publishable extension is the maximum-entropy network inference
(blueprint Project A) — infer the latent influence network and test whether it
beats geography at out-of-sample adoption forecasting.
